name: FBI JFK Cover Up
description: The "FBI JFK Cover Up" is a conspiracy theory alleging that the Federal Bureau of Investigation intentionally concealed or manipulated evidence related to the assassination of President John F. Kennedy in 1963. Proponents argue that the FBI's investigation, led by Director J. Edgar Hoover, was flawed and aimed at prematurely concluding Lee Harvey Oswald acted alone, thereby preventing a deeper examination of potential conspiracies. This theory is fueled by perceived inconsistencies in the Warren Commission report, the rapid sealing of JFK assassination records, and historical distrust of government agencies. Evidence cited includes alleged discrepancies in witness testimonies, suppressed information regarding Oswald's connections, and the FBI's handling of the Zapruder film. The mainstream scholarly perspective, supported by numerous historical analyses, generally acknowledges flaws in the initial investigation and information management, but attributes these to bureaucratic inefficiencies, political pressures, and the desire to quickly reassure a grieving nation rather than a deliberate conspiracy. Historians emphasize that while the FBI may have made mistakes or withheld information, there is no credible evidence to suggest a systematic effort to cover up a wider conspiracy involving the agency. The theory persists due to a complex interplay of factors, including public skepticism towards government institutions, the traumatic nature of the assassination, and the enduring appeal of conspiracy narratives.
subtitle: Alleged concealment of JFK assassination truths

claims.claim: The FBI suppressed evidence pointing to a wider conspiracy.
claims.evidence: Allegations of witness intimidation, selective reporting in the Warren Commission, and destruction of potential evidence.
claims.criticism: Mainstream historians argue that witness discrepancies are normal in traumatic events, and that supposed evidence destruction can be attributed to standard procedure or misinterpretation.

claims.claim: J. Edgar Hoover had a personal motive to cover up the truth.
claims.evidence: Hoover's alleged involvement in scandals and his desire to maintain the FBI's reputation, as well as rumored personal animosity towards the Kennedy family.
claims.criticism: While Hoover's questionable ethics are well-documented, no concrete evidence links these to a deliberate cover-up of the JFK assassination. His actions are often attributed to his authoritarian leadership style and desire to control information.

claims.claim: The FBI intentionally mishandled the Zapruder film.
claims.evidence: Claims of altered frames or selective presentation of the film to minimize evidence of multiple shooters or a conspiracy.
claims.criticism: Extensive analysis of the Zapruder film has been conducted, and while debate continues regarding its interpretation, there is no definitive proof of intentional FBI manipulation. The film's chain of custody has been thoroughly examined.

keyFigures.name: Mark Lane
keyFigures.role: Lawyer and author who challenged the Warren Commission report

keyFigures.name: G. Robert Blakey
keyFigures.role: Chief Counsel and Staff Director of the House Select Committee on Assassinations (HSCA)

keyFigures.name: Gerald Posner
keyFigures.role: Author of 'Case Closed,' a prominent critique of conspiracy theories

mythologyConnections: The archetype of the lone wolf vs. powerful cabal
The theme of hidden knowledge and suppressed truth

timeline.event: John F. Kennedy is assassinated in Dallas, Texas
Lee Harvey Oswald is shot and killed by Jack Ruby
The Warren Commission releases its report, concluding that Oswald acted alone
The HSCA concludes that there was a probable conspiracy, but does not identify the conspirators
The JFK Records Act is passed, mandating the release of previously classified documents
